subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Federal Government vs. States in Ensuring Educational Opportunities
stimulus: The federal government and the states each play an important role in public education. Take a position on whether the federal government or the states are more effective in ensuring educational opportunities for all students.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Fourteenth Amendment of the United States Constitution, Tenth Amendment of the United States Constitution, Article I of the United States Constitution.)
parts.scoring_notes: Thesis must take a clear position and establish a line of reasoning. Does NOT need foundational documents in the thesis.

scoring_points.description: Row A — Thesis/Claim: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: The federal government is more effective at ensuring educational opportunities for all students because the federal government has more resources to address issues of inequality than do individual states.

required_elements: Defensible claim establishing a line of reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: State governments are more effective at ensuring educational opportunities for all students because states are closer to citizens and can develop solutions to fit the particular issues that affect their communities.

wrong_examples: There is a debate about whether the federal or state governments should ensure educational equality. (Describes the debate — does not take a position)
Both federal and state governments play important roles in education. (No clear position on which is MORE effective)
common_traps: The thesis must choose between federal and state as 'more effective' — a middle-ground thesis that says both are important does not earn Row A
Foundational documents are NOT required in the thesis; citing the Fourteenth Amendment in the thesis is fine but not necessary for Row A
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row A (1 point): Responds to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. Examples that earn: 'The federal government is more effective at ensuring educational opportunities for all students because the federal government has more resources to address issues of inequality than do individual states.' 'The federal government is more effective at ensuring educational opportunities for all students because it can create national policies that apply to all the states.' 'State governments are more effective at ensuring educational opportunities for all students because states are closer to citizens and can develop solutions to fit the particular issues that affect the states.' Examples that do NOT earn: 'There is a debate about whether the federal or state governments should ensure educational equality.' (Restates prompt.) 'There are times when states do things that help their citizens out better than the national government.' (Does not respond to the prompt.)

sample_responses.response: The federal government is more effective at ensuring educational opportunities for all students because it has more resources to address issues of inequality than do individual states.
sample_responses.commentary: Takes a clear position (federal government) and provides a line of reasoning (greater resources to address inequality). Earns Row A.

sample_responses.response: State governments are more effective at ensuring educational opportunities because states are closer to citizens and can develop solutions tailored to their specific communities.
sample_responses.commentary: Clear position (state governments) with a line of reasoning (proximity to citizens, tailored solutions). Earns Row A.

sample_responses.response: There is a debate about whether the federal or state governments should ensure educational equality.
sample_responses.commentary: Restates the prompt without taking a position or establishing a line of reasoning. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
parts.scoring_notes: TIERED SCORING: Award the HIGHEST tier met. Tier 2 and 3 require Row A. Tier 3 requires at least one piece from the Fourteenth Amendment, Tenth Amendment, or Article I.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 1: Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt.

required_elements: One piece of evidence relevant to the topic
alternatives.correct_example: Mentions the Fourteenth Amendment, Tenth Amendment, Article I, Brown v. Board of Education, Every Student Succeeds Act, No Child Left Behind, or laboratories of democracy concept.

wrong_examples: Completely off-topic evidence unrelated to education or federal-state power
common_traps: Tier 1 does NOT require Row A and does NOT require supporting the thesis — mere relevance to the topic earns 1 point
Students may earn Tier 1 even with an inaccurate characterization of evidence, as long as it is topically relevant
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row B — 1 point: Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic of the prompt. May or may not have a claim or thesis. Relevant evidence includes: equal protection clause; laboratories of democracy; reserved powers; Civil Rights Act; Title IX; Brown v. Board of Education.

sample_responses.response: The Civil Rights Act of 1964 prohibited discrimination in public schools.
sample_responses.commentary: Relevant evidence about federal education policy. Earns Tier 1.

sample_responses.response: Parts of the Constitution support the idea that the federal government is more important than state governments.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague — not specific enough to count as a piece of evidence about education policy or federal-state powers. 0 pts.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 2: Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, OR provides two pieces of relevant evidence. Requires Row A.

required_elements: One specific piece that directly supports the thesis, OR two relevant pieces
alternatives.correct_example: Cites the Fourteenth Amendment's equal protection clause to support the argument that the federal government must ensure equal educational access across all states.

wrong_examples: Mentions the Tenth Amendment and Brown v. Board without explaining how either supports the thesis position on who should lead education policy
common_traps: Tier 2 REQUIRES Row A — no thesis means evidence is capped at 1 point
The connection between evidence and thesis must be made explicit — naming a document alone is not sufficient
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row B — 2 points: Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim or thesis. Requires Row A. Examples: 'The federal government can make national laws like Title IX of the Education Amendments Act to ensure that there is not discrimination on the basis of sex in educational policy.' 'States are useful as testing grounds for various policies that might provide equal access to educational opportunities prior to the federal government passing a national law.' 'The Federal government could use the equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment to require equal treatment in educational policy.' 'The reserved powers under the Tenth Amendment reserves the power to determine educational policy to the states.' 'Article I of the Constitution gives Congress the power to pass legislation that addresses education at the national level.'

sample_responses.response: The federal government can use the equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment to require states to provide equal educational opportunities, as demonstrated by the desegregation mandate from Brown v. Board of Education — directly supporting the thesis that the federal government is more effective.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses specific evidence (equal protection clause) from a listed document and connects it directly to the thesis. Earns Tier 2.

sample_responses.response: State governments share some powers with the federal government.
sample_responses.commentary: Not specific enough and not relevant to the question of who is more effective at ensuring educational opportunities. 0 pts.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Tier 3: Uses TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the thesis, at least one from a listed foundational document (Fourteenth Amendment, Tenth Amendment, or Article I). Requires Row A.

required_elements: Two specific pieces of evidence supporting the thesis
At least one from: Fourteenth Amendment, Tenth Amendment, or Article I
alternatives.correct_example: Uses the Tenth Amendment's reservation of powers to states to support the claim that states should lead education policy, AND cites the concept of 'laboratories of democracy' to show how states can innovate and share successful education reforms — both supporting the thesis.

wrong_examples: Uses Brown v. Board and the Civil Rights Act — both relevant and supporting the thesis but neither from the listed foundational documents
Cites the Fourteenth Amendment to argue states should control education (misapplies the amendment, which is more commonly associated with federal power to protect equal rights)
common_traps: The Tenth Amendment reserves powers not delegated to the federal government — it supports the argument that education (an unenumerated power) belongs to the states
The Fourteenth Amendment's equal protection clause supports federal intervention when states fail to provide equal educational opportunities — it supports the federal government position
Article I (necessary and proper clause, spending power) can support federal authority to condition education grants to states
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row B — 3 points: Uses two pieces of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim or thesis. One must come from a listed foundational document (Fourteenth Amendment, Tenth Amendment, or Article I). The other can come from a different foundational document or course concepts. Examples from foundational documents: 'The Federal government could use the equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment to require equal treatment in educational policy.' 'The reserved powers under the Tenth Amendment reserves the power to determine educational policy to the states.' 'Article I of the Constitution gives Congress the power to pass legislation that addresses education at the national level.'

sample_responses.response: The Tenth Amendment reserves unenumerated powers to the states, making education — not mentioned in the Constitution — a state responsibility. Additionally, states can serve as laboratories of democracy, testing different education policies before they are adopted more widely — both supporting the argument that states are more effective.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses the Tenth Amendment (a listed document) plus the laboratories of democracy concept (a course concept), both supporting the thesis. Earns Tier 3.

sample_responses.response: Brown v. Board of Education showed federal courts can ensure equal education. The Civil Rights Act of 1964 also required equal access to schools.
sample_responses.commentary: Two pieces of evidence supporting the federal government thesis but neither comes from the listed foundational documents (Fourteenth Amendment, Tenth Amendment, or Article I). Caps at Tier 2. 0 pts for Tier 3.

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A and at least 2 points in Row B. Must explain HOW or WHY the evidence supports the specific claim.

scoring_points.description: Row C — Reasoning: Use reasoning (classification, process, causation, or comparison) to explain how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim using causal or analytical logic
alternatives.correct_example: If a law passed by a state yields excellent results in education, that policy can be adopted by other states that believe it will also work for them, demonstrating how state-level experimentation leads to better education outcomes than a uniform federal mandate.

required_elements: Reasoning that connects evidence to claim using causal or analytical logic
alternatives.correct_example: The equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment has been used as a basis for the federal government to pass laws like the Civil Rights Act, which was remarkably effective in addressing educational inequality that states had perpetuated for decades.

wrong_examples: The Fourteenth Amendment is important for education. (States a fact without explaining the causal link to the thesis)
States know their communities better, so they should lead education policy. (General claim without connecting to specific evidence already cited)
common_traps: Reasoning must CONNECT the evidence already cited in Row B to the thesis — it is not an opportunity to introduce new evidence
Requires Row A AND Row B ≥ 2 pts — these must be earned first
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row C (1 point): Explains how or why the evidence supports the claim or thesis. Must explain the relationship between the evidence provided and the claim or thesis. Examples: 'If a law passed by a state yields excellent results, that policy can be adopted by other states that believe it will also work for them, and it can also be adopted by the federal government.' 'The equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment has been used as a basis for the federal government to pass laws such as the Civil Rights Act of 1964 which has been remarkably effective in addressing equality issues.' Requires Row A and at least 2 points in Row B.

sample_responses.response: The equal protection clause of the Fourteenth Amendment has been used as a basis for the federal government to pass laws such as the Civil Rights Act of 1964, which was remarkably effective at addressing educational inequality that states had perpetuated for decades through segregation. This shows why federal action is more effective — it creates uniform national standards that prevent states from discriminating.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains the causal chain from the Fourteenth Amendment evidence to why federal government action is more effective at ensuring educational equality. Earns Row C.

sample_responses.response: The Fourteenth Amendment and states' rights both show that education is an important issue.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not explain how the evidence supports the thesis — just notes that both are relevant. Does not show the causal or analytical connection. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
parts.scoring_notes: Requires Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND refute, concede, or rebut it.

scoring_points.description: Row D — Responds to Alternate Perspectives: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using rebuttal or refutation.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts, refutes, or concedes it with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: Some might argue that the federal government is more effective because it has greater resources, but federal laws are 'one size fits all' and might not always address the specific needs of diverse state communities and student populations.

required_elements: Describes alternate perspective AND rebuts, refutes, or concedes it with reasoning
alternatives.correct_example: While some argue that states are better suited to ensure educational equality because they know local needs, state governments also have a long history of discriminatory education policies, which requires federal intervention to ensure equal opportunity for all students.

wrong_examples: Some argue that states' rights should take priority. (Only identifies the alternate perspective — no rebuttal, concession, or refutation)
Some people disagree with federal control of education. (Too vague — does not describe a substantive alternate perspective or engage with it)
common_traps: Must both DESCRIBE the alternate view AND RESPOND to it — students who do only one of these do not earn Row D
Requires Row A — a student without a thesis cannot earn Row D
scoring_points.official_rubric: Row D (1 point): Responds to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal. Must describe an alternate perspective AND refute, concede, or rebut it. Requires Row A. Examples that earn: 'Some might argue that the federal government is more effective because it has greater resources, but federal laws are "one size fits all" and might not always address the specific needs of states and communities.' 'While some argue that states are better suited to ensure educational equality because they are better equipped to meet local needs, state governments also have a long history of discrimination against minorities through Jim Crow laws and other segregationist policies, which requires the federal government to establish uniform policy to ensure equality in education across the states.' Does NOT earn: 'Some argue that states' rights should take priority.' (Identifies but does not rebut.) 'Some people say that the federal government is better at ensuring educational equality but that is just flat out wrong.' (Dismissal without substantive engagement.)

sample_responses.response: Some argue that the federal government is more effective because it has greater financial resources to address educational inequality. However, federal laws apply uniformly across all states and may not address the specific needs of diverse communities — a state-designed solution can be tailored to local demographics and challenges in ways a national mandate cannot.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies the opposing view (federal resources) and rebuts it by explaining the one-size-fits-all limitation of federal laws. Engages substantively. Earns Row D.

sample_responses.response: Some argue that states are better at ensuring educational equality, but that is simply not true.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies an alternate perspective but dismisses it without substantive engagement — does not explain why it is wrong. 0 pts.

sample_responses.response: While some argue that states are better suited to ensure equal education because they know local needs, state governments have a long history of discriminatory education policies — from school segregation under Jim Crow to unequal school funding — which demonstrates why federal intervention is necessary to ensure equal opportunity for all students regardless of where they live.
sample_responses.commentary: Concedes the local knowledge advantage of states but rebuts it with the historical record of state discrimination — directly engages the specific argument for state control. Earns Row D.
